1.1: The proverbs of Solomon, son of David, king of Israel:
1.2: For learning wisdom and discipline, for understanding insightful words;
1.3: To gain training in wise conduct — in righteousness, justice, and integrity;
1.4: To give shrewdness to the naive and knowledge and discernment to the young —
1.5: Let the wise hear and grow in learning; let the discerning acquire skill in guidance,
1.6: To understand a proverb and its meaning, the words of the wise and their enigmas.
1.7: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge; fools despise wisdom and discipline.
1.8: Listen, my son, to your father's instruction, and do not abandon your mother's teaching;
1.9: They will be a graceful garland on your head and a chain of honor around your neck.
1.10: My son, if sinners entice you, do not yield.
1.11: If they say, 'Come with us — let us lie in wait for someone's blood, let us ambush the innocent for no reason;'
1.12: We will swallow them alive like Sheol, whole like those who descend to the grave;
1.13: We will find every kind of treasure and fill our homes with loot;
1.14: Throw in your lot with us; we will share a common purse —
1.15: My son, do not walk on their road; keep your feet far from their path;
1.16: Their feet run toward evil and they rush to shed blood.
1.17: How futile to spread a net in full view of the bird you intend to catch.
1.18: These men lie in wait for their own blood; they ambush their own lives.
1.19: Such is the fate of everyone who is greedy for unjust gain — it costs them their lives.
1.20: Wisdom cries out in the streets; in the public squares she raises her voice;
1.21: At the head of the busy thoroughfares she calls; at the gates of the city she speaks:
1.22: How long, you naive ones, will you love foolishness? How long will scoffers delight in their scorn and fools hate knowledge?
1.23: Turn back at my rebuke. Look — I will pour out my spirit to you; I will make my words known to you.
1.24: But I called and you refused; I stretched out my hand and no one paid attention;
1.25: You rejected all my advice and would accept none of my correction —
1.26: I will laugh at your calamity; I will mock when terror strikes you,
1.27: When terror sweeps over you like a storm and your calamity arrives like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish overwhelm you.
1.28: Then they will call on me, but I will not answer; they will search for me but will not find me.
1.29: Because they hated knowledge and did not choose to fear the LORD,
1.30: They wanted none of my advice and rejected every correction I offered.
1.31: So they will eat the fruit of their own path and be gorged on their own schemes.
1.32: The waywardness of the naive will kill them, and the complacency of fools will be their ruin;
1.33: But whoever listens to me will live in security and be free from the dread of harm.

2.1: My son, if you accept my words and store up my commands within you,
2.2: Turning your ear toward wisdom and directing your heart to understanding,
2.3: Yes, if you call out for discernment and cry aloud for understanding,
2.4: If you search for it like silver and dig for it like buried treasure,
2.5: Then you will understand what it means to fear the LORD and you will find the knowledge of God.
2.6: For the LORD gives wisdom; from his mouth flow knowledge and understanding.
2.7: He stores up proven wisdom for the upright; he is a shield for those who live with integrity,
2.8: He guards the paths of justice and protects the way of those who are faithful to him.
2.9: Then you will understand what is right and just and fair — every good way of living.
2.10: For wisdom will enter your heart and knowledge will delight your inner being;
2.11: Discretion will watch over you; understanding will protect you,
2.12: Delivering you from the path of evil and from those who speak twisted things,
2.13: Who abandon the straight paths and choose to walk in darkness,
2.14: Who take pleasure in evil and celebrate every kind of wrongdoing,
2.15: Their ways are crooked and their paths are twisted.
2.16: It will also save you from the immoral woman — from the seductive stranger with her smooth words,
2.17: She has abandoned the partner of her youth and forgotten the covenant she made before God.
2.18: For her house leads down to death and her paths go to the spirits of the dead.
2.19: None who enter her house return; they never find their way back to the paths of life.
2.20: All this so that you will walk in the path of the good and stay on the road of the righteous.
2.21: For the righteous will live in the land, and those of integrity will remain in it,
2.22: But the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the unfaithful will be uprooted from it.

3.1: My son, do not forget my instruction, but let your heart hold on to my commands;
3.2: They will add to you a long life, many years of living, and peace.
3.3: Let steadfast love and faithfulness never leave you; wear them like a necklace; inscribe them on your heart.
3.4: Then you will find favor and a good reputation in the eyes of God and people.
3.5: Trust in the LORD with all your heart and do not depend on your own insight.
3.6: Acknowledge him in everything you do, and he will direct your steps.
3.7: Do not think of yourself as wise; fear the LORD and turn from evil.
3.8: This will bring health to your whole body and strength to your bones.
3.9: Honor the LORD with your wealth and with the first portion of all you produce;
3.10: Then your barns will overflow with abundance and your wine vats will brim with new wine.
3.11: My son, do not reject the LORD's discipline or resent his correction;
3.12: For the LORD corrects those he loves, just as a father disciplines the son he takes delight in.
3.13: How happy is the person who finds wisdom, the one who gains understanding;
3.14: Her return is better than the return on silver, and her profit better than fine gold;
3.15: She is more valuable than precious gems; nothing you could want measures up to her.
3.16: In her right hand is long life; in her left hand are wealth and honor.
3.17: Her ways are pleasant and all her paths lead to peace.
3.18: She is a tree of life to those who grasp her; those who hold on to her are called blessed.
3.19: By wisdom the LORD laid the foundation of the earth; by understanding he set the heavens in place.
3.20: Through his knowledge the deep waters opened up and the clouds let fall their dew.
3.21: My son, keep these in view; hold on to sound wisdom and discernment;
3.22: They will bring life to your soul and beauty to your neck.
3.23: Then you will go on your way in security, without stumbling.
3.24: When you lie down, you will not be afraid; your sleep will be peaceful.
3.25: Do not fear sudden catastrophe or the destruction that falls on the wicked;
3.26: For the LORD will be your security and will keep you from every snare.
3.27: Do not withhold good from those who deserve it when you have the ability to give it.
3.28: Do not tell your neighbor, 'Come back later — I'll help you tomorrow,' when you can help now.
3.29: Do not plot harm against your neighbor, who lives beside you in trust.
3.30: Do not pick a fight without cause with someone who has done you no harm.
3.31: Do not envy the violent person or choose to follow any of his paths;
3.32: For the LORD detests the devious, but the upright are in his confidence.
3.33: The LORD's curse rests on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the home of the righteous.
3.34: He mocks the mockers himself, but shows favor to the humble.
3.35: The wise will inherit honor, but fools are left with disgrace.

7.1: My son, keep my words and store up my commands within you.
7.2: Keep my commands and live; guard my teaching as the apple of your eye.
7.3: Tie them on your fingers; inscribe them on the tablet of your heart.
7.4: Say to wisdom, 'You are my sister,' and call understanding your closest companion,
7.5: They will keep you from the immoral woman, from the seductive stranger with her flattering words.
7.6: Once at the window of my house I looked out through the lattice,
7.7: I observed among the naive, among the young men, one who lacked all sense —
7.8: Cutting through the street near her corner, heading down the road toward her house,
7.9: This was in the twilight, in the evening, in the black darkness of night.
7.10: A woman met him, dressed like a prostitute, her heart scheming.
7.11: She is loud and defiant; her feet never stay at home;
7.12: Now outside, now in the streets — lurking at every corner.
7.13: She grabbed him and kissed him and said to him with a brazen face:
7.14: 'I had fellowship offerings to make; today I paid my vows.
7.15: 'That is why I came out to meet you — I was looking for you and here you are.
7.16: 'I have spread my bed with colored blankets and fine linen from Egypt.
7.17: 'I have scented my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
7.18: 'Come, let us drink our fill of love until morning; let us delight in lovemaking.
7.19: 'My husband is not home; he has gone on a long trip;
7.20: 'He took his money with him; he will not be back until the full moon.'
7.21: With her many persuasive words she led him astray; with the smooth speech of her lips she compelled him.
7.22: He followed her at once — like an ox heading to slaughter, like a fool walking into punishment,
7.23: Until an arrow pierces his liver — like a bird rushing into a snare, not knowing that his life is the price.
7.24: So now, my children, listen to me — pay close attention to what I am saying.
7.25: Do not let your heart wander toward her ways; do not go astray on her paths.
7.26: She has brought down many victims; a long line of powerful men have been destroyed by her.
7.27: Her house is the road to Sheol; it leads to the inner chambers of death.

8.1: Does wisdom not call out? Does understanding not raise her voice?
8.2: She takes her stand on the high ground, at the crossroads where the paths meet.
8.3: She cries out at the city gates, at the entrance of the city, at the doorways.
8.4: 'I am calling to you — to all humanity, to every child of Adam.
8.5: 'You naive ones, learn prudence; you fools, gain a discerning heart.
8.6: 'Listen — I am speaking what is excellent; everything from my mouth will be right.
8.7: 'My mouth speaks truth; wickedness is detestable to me.
8.8: 'Every word I speak is right; nothing I say is twisted or deceptive.
8.9: 'To those with understanding, my words are all clear; to those who have found knowledge, they are entirely right.
8.10: 'Take my instruction rather than silver, and choose knowledge over the finest gold.
8.11: 'Wisdom is more valuable than precious gems; nothing you could wish for compares to her.
8.12: 'I, wisdom, live alongside prudence, and find knowledge and good judgment.
8.13: 'The fear of the LORD means hating evil — I hate pride, arrogance, evil conduct, and perverse speech.
8.14: 'Mine are counsel and tested wisdom; I am understanding; I have strength.
8.15: 'Through me kings reign and rulers issue just decrees.
8.16: 'Through me princes govern, and nobles — all who exercise righteous authority over the earth.
8.17: 'I love those who love me; those who seek me earnestly will find me.
8.18: 'With me are riches and honor — lasting wealth and righteousness.
8.19: 'What I produce is better than gold — even fine gold; my yield surpasses choice silver.
8.20: 'I walk in the path of righteousness, along the roads of justice,
8.21: 'Giving a real inheritance to those who love me, and filling their storehouses.
8.22: 'The LORD possessed me at the beginning of his way, before the oldest of his works.
8.23: 'I was installed from eternity — from the very beginning, before the earth existed.
8.24: 'Before the oceans existed I was born — before the springs were teeming with water.
8.25: 'Before the mountains were in place, before the hills, I was brought into being —
8.26: 'Before he had made the earth or its open lands or the first dust of the world.
8.27: 'When he set the heavens in place I was there; when he drew the horizon over the face of the deep;
8.28: 'When he fixed the clouds above and set firm the fountains of the deep;
8.29: 'When he gave the sea its limits so the waters could not overstep his command, when he marked out the earth's foundations —
8.30: 'Then I was beside him, the master craftsman; I was his daily delight, rejoicing before him always,
8.31: 'Rejoicing in the world he had made, and my delight was in the children of humanity.
8.32: 'So now, my children, listen to me — blessed are those who keep to my ways.
8.33: 'Accept instruction and be wise; do not reject it.
8.34: 'Blessed is the person who listens to me — waiting at my doors every morning, watching at my threshold.
8.35: 'Whoever finds me finds life and receives the LORD's favor.
8.36: 'But whoever fails to find me harms himself; all who hate me embrace death.'

9.1: Wisdom has built her house; she has hewn out her seven pillars.
9.2: She has slaughtered her animals, mixed her wine, and set her table.
9.3: She has sent out her servants, calling from the heights of the city:
9.4: 'Let the simple come in here.' To the one who lacks understanding she says,
9.5: 'Come and eat my bread; drink the wine I have mixed.
9.6: 'Leave your simple ways and live; walk in the path of understanding.
9.7: Whoever rebukes a mocker earns insults; whoever corrects a wicked man gets hurt.
9.8: Do not rebuke a mocker or he will only hate you; rebuke a wise person and he will love you for it.
9.9: Teach the wise and they grow wiser still; instruct the righteous and they gain more.
9.10: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom; knowing the Holy One is understanding.
9.11: For through me your days will multiply and years will be added to your life.
9.12: If you are wise, the benefit is yours alone; if you scoff, you alone bear the consequences.
9.13: Dame Folly is loud; she is naive and knows nothing.
9.14: She sits at the door of her house, in a seat at the high places of the city,
9.15: Calling out to those who are passing by, going straight on their way:
9.16: 'Let the simple come in here.' To the one who lacks understanding she says,
9.17: 'Stolen water is sweet, and food eaten in secret is delicious.'
9.18: But he does not know that the dead are there — that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.

4.1: Hear, my children, a father's instruction, and pay attention so that you may gain understanding,
4.2: For I give you sound teaching; do not abandon my instruction.
4.3: For when I was a son, tender in age and the only child in my mother's eyes,
4.4: He taught me and said: 'Hold my words in your heart; keep my commands and you will live.'
4.5: Get wisdom, get understanding; do not forget or turn away from my words.
4.6: Do not abandon wisdom and she will protect you; love her and she will guard you.
4.7: Getting wisdom is the most important thing — so pursue wisdom; and along with everything else you acquire, gain understanding.
4.8: Honor her and she will honor you; embrace her and she will lift you up.
4.9: She will give you a graceful wreath for your head and place a glorious crown on you.
4.10: Listen, my son, accept my words, and the years of your life will be multiplied.
4.11: I have instructed you in the way of wisdom; I have guided you along straight paths.
4.12: When you walk, your path will not be obstructed; when you run, you will not trip.
4.13: Hold tightly to instruction; do not let her go — guard her, for she is your life.
4.14: Do not set foot on the path of the wicked or walk in the way of evil people.
4.15: Shun it; do not travel it; turn away from it and keep going.
4.16: They cannot sleep unless they have done wrong; their sleep is taken from them unless they have made someone stumble.
4.17: For they feed on wickedness and drink violence like wine.
4.18: The path of the righteous is like the light of dawn — it shines brighter and brighter until midday.
4.19: The way of the wicked is like deep darkness; they do not know what makes them fall.
4.20: My son, pay attention to my words; turn your ear toward my teaching.
4.21: Do not lose sight of them; hold them at the center of your heart.
4.22: For they are life to those who discover them and health to their whole body.
4.23: Guard your heart above all else, for it is the source from which life flows.
4.24: Rid yourself of corrupt speech; keep dishonest talk far from your lips.
4.25: Let your eyes look forward, with your gaze fixed straight ahead.
4.26: Carefully consider the path you walk, and make all your ways steady.
4.27: Do not swerve to the right or the left; keep your feet away from evil.

5.1: My son, pay attention to my wisdom; turn your ear toward my understanding,
5.2: So that you will hold on to discernment and your lips will guard knowledge.
5.3: For the lips of the immoral woman drip honey, and her words are smoother than oil;
5.4: But in the end she is as bitter as wormwood, as sharp as a two-edged sword.
5.5: Her feet go down to death; her steps lead straight to Sheol.
5.6: She does not consider the path of life; her ways wander and she does not realize it.
5.7: So now, my sons, listen to me; do not turn away from what I am telling you.
5.8: Keep your road far from her; do not go near the door of her house,
5.9: Or you will surrender your dignity to others and your prime years to those who are ruthless,
5.10: And strangers will consume your prosperity, and your hard work will end up in a foreigner's house,
5.11: And at the end you will groan when your body is wasted and your health is gone,
5.12: And you will say: 'How I hated correction, and how my heart despised being rebuked!
5.13: I would not heed the voice of my teachers or turn my ear toward those who instructed me.
5.14: I was on the verge of complete ruin in the sight of the whole community.'
5.15: Drink water from your own cistern and fresh water from your own well.
5.16: Should your springs be scattered in the streets, your streams of water in the public squares?
5.17: Let them be yours alone, not shared with strangers.
5.18: May your source of life be blessed, and may you find joy with the wife of your youth.
5.19: A loving deer, a graceful doe — may her love satisfy you completely at all times; may you always be captivated by her love.
5.20: Why, my son, would you be captivated by an immoral woman or hold close a stranger?
5.21: For a man's ways are in plain sight before the LORD, and he examines every path he takes.
5.22: The wicked man is ensnared by his own iniquities and held fast by the ropes of his own sin.
5.23: He dies for lack of discipline and is led astray by his immense foolishness.

6.1: My son, if you have become surety for your neighbor, if you have given your hand in a pledge for a stranger,
6.2: You are snared by your own words; you are trapped by what you have said.
6.3: Do this at once, my son, and free yourself, since you have fallen into your neighbor's hand: go, humble yourself, and urgently plead with your neighbor.
6.4: Do not let your eyes sleep or your eyelids slumber.
6.5: Save yourself like a gazelle escaping from the hunter's hand, like a bird from the hand of the trapper.
6.6: Go to the ant, you sluggard; observe her ways and become wise.
6.7: With no commander, no overseer, and no master above her,
6.8: She stores her provisions in summer and gathers her food at harvest time.
6.9: How long will you lie there, you sluggard? When will you get up from your sleep?
6.10: 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest' —
6.11: And poverty will come on you like a thief and scarcity like an armed man.
6.12: A scoundrel, a wicked man, goes about with corrupt speech;
6.13: He communicates with his eyes, shuffles his feet, and gestures with his fingers;
6.14: With a corrupt mind he constantly plots evil and stirs up conflict.
6.15: Therefore disaster will strike him suddenly; in an instant he will be shattered beyond recovery.
6.16: There are six things the LORD hates, and seven that are detestable to him:
6.17: Proud eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that kill the innocent,
6.18: A mind that plots wicked plans and feet that run swiftly toward evil,
6.19: A false witness who spreads lies, and a person who stirs up conflict among brothers.
6.20: My son, keep your father's command and do not abandon your mother's instruction.
6.21: Bind them to your heart at all times; fasten them around your neck.
6.22: When you travel, they will guide you; when you sleep, they will guard you; and when you wake, they will speak to you.
6.23: For the command is a lamp and the teaching is a light, and the corrections of discipline are the pathway to life,
6.24: To protect you from the immoral woman and from the flattering tongue of the adulteress.
6.25: Do not desire her beauty in your heart, and do not be taken captive by her alluring eyes.
6.26: For a prostitute reduces a man to a loaf of bread, but an adulteress preys on his very life.
6.27: Can a man carry fire against his chest without his clothes catching fire?
6.28: Can a person walk on hot coals without scorching his feet?
6.29: So it is with the man who sleeps with his neighbor's wife; no one who touches her will escape punishment.
6.30: People do not despise a thief who steals because he is starving.
6.31: But if he is caught, he must repay seven times over, giving up everything in his house.
6.32: But a man who commits adultery has no sense; whoever does it destroys his own life.
6.33: He will suffer wounds and dishonor, and his shame will never be erased.
6.34: For jealousy enrages a husband, and he will show no mercy when he takes his revenge.
6.35: He will accept no compensation; he will refuse every bribe, no matter how large.

10.1: The proverbs of Solomon: a wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son brings grief to his mother.
10.2: Ill-gotten wealth profits nothing, but righteousness delivers from death.
10.3: The LORD does not let the righteous go hungry, but he thwarts the craving of the wicked.
10.4: A slack hand leads to poverty, but the hand of the diligent brings wealth.
10.5: A wise son gathers crops in summer, but a son who sleeps through the harvest brings shame.
10.6: Blessings crown the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
10.7: The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked rots.
10.8: The wise in heart accepts instruction, but the babbling fool will come to ruin.
10.9: Whoever walks with integrity walks in security, but whoever twists his ways will be exposed.
10.10: The one who winks with malice stirs up trouble, but the babbling fool will come to ruin.
10.11: The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
10.12: Hatred stirs up conflict, but love covers all offenses.
10.13: On the lips of the discerning, wisdom is found; but a rod belongs on the back of the one who lacks good sense.
10.14: Wise people store up knowledge, but the mouth of a fool brings destruction near.
10.15: A wealthy person's riches are like a fortified city to him; poverty is the ruin of the poor.
10.16: The earnings of the righteous lead to life, but the income of the wicked leads to sin.
10.17: Whoever keeps instruction is on the path of life, but whoever abandons correction goes astray.
10.18: Whoever conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever spreads slander is a fool.
10.19: When words are many, sin is not far off, but whoever holds back his words is prudent.
10.20: The tongue of the righteous is like choice silver; the heart of the wicked has little value.
10.21: The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die because they lack good judgment.
10.22: The blessing of the LORD is what truly makes rich, and he adds no sorrow to it.
10.23: Doing wrong is like a game to a fool, but wisdom is the delight of a person of understanding.
10.24: What the wicked fears will come upon him, but the longing of the righteous will be fulfilled.
10.25: When the storm passes, the wicked are gone, but the righteous stand on an everlasting foundation.
10.26: As vinegar is to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is a lazy person to those who employ him.
10.27: The fear of the LORD adds length to life, but the years of the wicked are cut short.
10.28: The hope of the righteous brings joy, but the expectation of the wicked comes to nothing.
10.29: The LORD's way is a stronghold for the blameless, but it means ruin for those who do evil.
10.30: The righteous will never be dislodged, but the wicked will not remain in the land.
10.31: The mouth of the righteous produces wisdom, but the perverse tongue will be cut off.
10.32: The lips of the righteous know what is pleasing, but the mouth of the wicked speaks only what is perverse.

11.1: Dishonest scales are detestable to the LORD, but an accurate weight is his delight.
11.2: When pride arrives, disgrace follows, but with the humble comes wisdom.
11.3: The integrity of the upright guides them, but the treachery of the faithless destroys them.
11.4: Wealth is of no use on the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death.
11.5: The righteousness of the blameless makes his way straight, but the wicked fall by their own wickedness.
11.6: The righteousness of the upright rescues them, but the treacherous are trapped by their own evil desires.
11.7: When a wicked man dies, his expectation perishes, and the hope placed in wrongdoers comes to nothing.
11.8: The righteous are rescued from trouble, and the wicked take their place in it.
11.9: Through his words the godless person destroys his neighbor, but the righteous are saved through knowledge.
11.10: When the righteous prosper, the city rejoices, and when the wicked perish, there are shouts of joy.
11.11: By the blessing of the upright the city is lifted up, but by the mouth of the wicked it is torn down.
11.12: Whoever despises his neighbor lacks good sense, but a man of understanding stays silent.
11.13: Whoever goes about gossiping reveals secrets, but a person of faithful spirit keeps confidence.
11.14: Without guidance, a people falls, but with many counselors comes safety.
11.15: Whoever gives surety for a stranger will suffer harm, but whoever refuses such pledges is safe.
11.16: A gracious woman gains honor, but ruthless men gain only wealth.
11.17: A kind man benefits himself, but the cruel person causes harm to himself.
11.18: The wicked earns deceptive wages, but whoever sows righteousness gains a sure reward.
11.19: Genuine righteousness leads to life, but whoever pursues evil runs toward his own death.
11.20: Those with crooked hearts are detestable to the LORD, but those whose ways are blameless are his delight.
11.21: Be assured: the wicked will not go unpunished, but the offspring of the righteous will escape.
11.22: Like a gold ring in a pig's snout is a beautiful woman who lacks discretion.
11.23: The desire of the righteous ends in good, but the hope of the wicked brings wrath.
11.24: One person gives freely and grows richer, while another withholds what he should give and only grows poorer.
11.25: A generous person will be enriched, and whoever waters others will himself be watered.
11.26: The people curse whoever hoards grain, but blessings fall on the head of whoever sells it.
11.27: Whoever earnestly seeks good will gain favor, but evil comes upon whoever pursues it.
11.28: Whoever trusts in his wealth will fall, but the righteous will flourish like a fresh branch.
11.29: Whoever brings trouble on his own household will inherit the wind, and the fool will end up serving the wise of heart.
11.30: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and whoever wins people to wisdom is wise.
11.31: If the righteous receive what they deserve on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner!

12.1: Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but whoever hates correction is without understanding.
12.2: A good man obtains favor from the LORD, but a man who schemes wickedly he condemns.
12.3: No one is made secure by wickedness, but the root of the righteous will not be shaken.
12.4: An excellent wife is a crown to her husband, but she who brings shame is like decay in his bones.
12.5: The plans of the righteous are just, but the schemes of the wicked are deceitful.
12.6: The words of the wicked set an ambush for blood, but the mouth of the upright delivers people.
12.7: The wicked are overthrown and are gone, but the house of the righteous stands firm.
12.8: A man is praised according to his wisdom, but one with a twisted mind is despised.
12.9: It is better to be of humble standing and have food than to put on airs and lack bread.
12.10: A righteous man cares for the wellbeing of his animal, but even the compassion of the wicked is cruel.
12.11: Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but whoever chases empty pursuits lacks good sense.
12.12: The wicked crave what evil people catch, but the root of the righteous produces its own fruit.
12.13: An evil man is trapped by the sins of his lips, but the righteous escapes from trouble.
12.14: From the fruit of his words a man is satisfied with good, and the work of his hands comes back to him.
12.15: The path of a fool seems right to him, but a wise person listens to counsel.
12.16: A fool's annoyance is known at once, but a prudent person overlooks an insult.
12.17: A truthful witness gives honest testimony, but a false witness speaks lies.
12.18: Reckless words pierce like a sword, but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
12.19: Truthful lips stand firm forever, but a lying tongue lasts only a moment.
12.20: Deceit fills the heart of those who plan evil, but those who counsel peace find joy.
12.21: No harm happens to the righteous, but the wicked are filled with trouble.
12.22: Lying lips are detestable to the LORD, but those who act faithfully are his delight.
12.23: A prudent man conceals what he knows, but the heart of a fool broadcasts its folly.
12.24: The diligent hand will rule, but the lazy will be put to forced labor.
12.25: Anxiety in a man's heart weighs him down, but a good word makes him glad.
12.26: The righteous man guides his neighbor well, but the way of the wicked leads them astray.
12.27: The lazy man does not even bother to prepare his game, but the wealth of a diligent man is a precious thing.
12.28: In the path of righteousness is life, and along that road there is no death.

19.1: Better is a poor man who walks in his integrity than one who is crooked in speech and is a fool.
19.2: Desire without knowledge is not good, and the one who moves too fast misses the way.
19.3: A man's own foolishness leads his way astray, yet his heart rages against the LORD.
19.4: Wealth attracts many friends, but the poor man is separated from his neighbor.
19.5: A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever speaks lies will not escape.
19.6: Many seek the favor of a generous man, and everyone is a friend to the one who gives gifts.
19.7: All the brothers of a poor man hate him — how much more do his friends keep their distance. He may call after them, but they are gone.
19.8: Whoever acquires wisdom loves himself; whoever holds to understanding will find what is good.
19.9: A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever breathes out lies will perish.
19.10: Luxury does not suit a fool, and it is even worse for a slave to rule over princes.
19.11: A man's wisdom makes him slow to anger, and it is his glory to overlook an offense.
19.12: A king's wrath is like the roaring of a lion, but his favor is like dew upon the grass.
19.13: A foolish son is ruin to his father, and a wife's quarreling is like a constant drip.
19.14: A house and wealth can be inherited from one's father, but a sensible wife comes from the LORD.
19.15: Laziness brings on a deep sleep, and the idle person will go hungry.
19.16: Whoever keeps the commandment keeps his life, but whoever is careless about his conduct will die.
19.17: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD, and he will repay him for his deed.
19.18: Discipline your son while there is still hope; do not indulge him to the point of his destruction.
19.19: A man of great wrath will bear the penalty; if you rescue him, you will only have to do it again.
19.20: Listen to counsel and accept instruction, that you may gain wisdom in the end.
19.21: Many are the plans in a man's heart, but the LORD's purpose is what stands.
19.22: What is desired in a person is faithful love, and a poor man is better than a liar.
19.23: The fear of the LORD leads to life; whoever has it will rest satisfied and will not be touched by evil.
19.24: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish and will not even bring it to his mouth again.
19.25: Strike a mocker and the naive will gain caution; correct a man of understanding and he gains knowledge.
19.26: A son who mistreats his father and drives his mother away brings shame and disgrace.
19.27: Stop listening to instruction, my son, and you will drift from words of knowledge.
19.28: A corrupt witness mocks justice, and the mouth of the wicked gulps down iniquity.
19.29: Penalties are set in store for mockers, and beatings for the backs of fools.

20.1: Wine is a mocker and strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise.
20.2: The terror of a king is like the roaring of a lion; whoever provokes him to anger puts his own life at risk.
20.3: It is an honor for a man to stay out of conflict, but every fool will be drawn into quarreling.
20.4: The sluggard does not plow in the cold season; at harvest he will look for food and find nothing.
20.5: The counsel in a man's heart is like deep water, but a man of understanding draws it out.
20.6: Many people proclaim their own loyalty, but a truly faithful person — who can find?
20.7: A righteous man who walks in his integrity — blessed are his children after him.
20.8: A king who sits on the throne of judgment scatters all evil with his eyes.
20.9: Who can say, 'I have made my heart pure; I am clean from my sin'?
20.10: Unequal weights and unequal measures — both are detestable to the LORD.
20.11: Even a child is known by his actions, whether his conduct is pure and right.
20.12: The hearing ear and the seeing eye — the LORD made them both.
20.13: Do not love sleep, or you will come to poverty; keep your eyes open and you will have plenty of food.
20.14: Bad, bad, says the buyer, but when he goes away, he boasts of his deal.
20.15: There is gold and plenty of rubies, but the lips that speak knowledge are the truly precious thing.
20.16: Take the garment of one who puts up security for a stranger; hold it in pledge for those who are surety for foreigners.
20.17: Bread gained by deceit is sweet to a man, but afterward his mouth will be full of gravel.
20.18: Plans are established by seeking advice, and with wise guidance you can wage war.
20.19: The gossip reveals secrets wherever he goes; avoid anyone who talks too freely.
20.20: Whoever curses his father or his mother — his lamp will be snuffed out in deepest darkness.
20.21: An inheritance quickly gained at first will not be blessed in the end.
20.22: Do not say, 'I will repay evil'; wait on the LORD and he will deliver you.
20.23: Unequal weights are detestable to the LORD, and dishonest scales are no good.
20.24: A man's steps are directed by the LORD; how then can anyone understand his own path?
20.25: It is a snare for a man to dedicate something rashly, and only afterward to reconsider.
20.26: A wise king scatters the wicked and drives the threshing wheel over them.
20.27: The human spirit is the LORD's lamp, searching all the innermost parts of a person.
20.28: Steadfast love and faithfulness preserve the king, and his throne is upheld by faithful love.
20.29: The glory of young men is their strength, but the honor of old men is their gray hair.
20.30: Painful blows cleanse away evil; wounds reach the innermost parts of a person.

21.1: The king's heart is in the hand of the LORD like channels of water; he directs it wherever he pleases.
21.2: Every man thinks his own way is right, but the LORD weighs the heart.
21.3: To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice.
21.4: Haughty eyes and a proud heart — even the tillage of the wicked — are sin.
21.5: The plans of the diligent lead to abundance, but hasty action leads only to poverty.
21.6: Treasures gained by a lying tongue are a fleeting vapor — a deadly snare.
21.7: The violence of the wicked will drag them away, because they refuse to do what is right.
21.8: The way of the guilty person is crooked and strange, but the conduct of the pure is upright.
21.9: It is better to live in a corner of the roof than in a spacious house with a quarrelsome woman.
21.10: The wicked desire evil; they show no kindness even to their neighbor.
21.11: When a mocker is punished, the naive become wise; when a wise person receives instruction, he gains knowledge.
21.12: The righteous perceive the house of the wicked; God brings the wicked down to ruin.
21.13: Whoever stops his ears at the cry of the poor will himself cry out and not be heard.
21.14: A secret gift calms anger, and a concealed bribe soothes fierce wrath.
21.15: It is a joy to the righteous to do justice, but it brings terror to evildoers.
21.16: Whoever wanders from the way of understanding will come to rest among the dead.
21.17: Whoever loves pleasure will be a poor man; whoever loves wine and olive oil will not grow rich.
21.18: The wicked are a substitute for the righteous, and the treacherous take the place of the upright.
21.19: It is better to live in a desert than with a quarrelsome and angry woman.
21.20: Precious treasure and oil are stored in the house of the wise, but a fool quickly squanders them.
21.21: Whoever pursues righteousness and steadfast love will find life, righteousness, and honor.
21.22: A wise man can take a city of warriors and bring down the stronghold in which they trust.
21.23: Whoever guards his mouth and tongue keeps himself out of trouble.
21.24: Proud, haughty, 'Scoffer' is his name — he acts with overweening arrogance.
21.25: The craving of a sluggard will be his death, because his hands refuse to work.
21.26: The wicked covet all day long, but the righteous give generously without holding back.
21.27: The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable; how much more when offered with evil intent.
21.28: A false witness will perish, but whoever listens speaks truthfully and endures.
21.29: A wicked man puts on a bold face, but the upright person carefully considers his way.
21.30: There is no wisdom, no understanding, no counsel that can stand against the LORD.
21.31: The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but victory belongs to the LORD.

13.1: A wise son heeds his father's instruction, but a mocker does not listen to rebuke.
13.2: A man eats well from the fruit of his words, but the soul of the treacherous craves violence.
13.3: Whoever guards his mouth protects his life, but whoever speaks without restraint will come to ruin.
13.4: The soul of a lazy person craves and gets nothing, but the diligent soul is richly satisfied.
13.5: A righteous man hates falsehood, but a wicked man acts disgracefully and brings shame.
13.6: Righteousness guards the one who walks uprightly, but wickedness brings down the sinner.
13.7: One person acts rich and has nothing; another acts poor yet has great wealth.
13.8: A man's wealth may ransom his life, but a poor man hears no threatening.
13.9: The light of the righteous shines with joy, but the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished.
13.10: Pride produces nothing but conflict, but wisdom is found in those who take counsel.
13.11: Wealth gained by fraud will shrink, but whoever gathers it gradually through labor will see it grow.
13.12: Hope deferred makes the heart grow sick, but a longing fulfilled is a tree of life.
13.13: Whoever despises instruction will be brought to ruin, but whoever respects the commandment will be rewarded.
13.14: The teaching of the wise is a fountain of life, turning one away from the traps of death.
13.15: Good understanding wins favor, but the way of the treacherous leads to their ruin.
13.16: Every prudent person acts from knowledge, but a fool broadcasts his foolishness.
13.17: A wicked messenger causes harm, but a trustworthy envoy brings healing.
13.18: Poverty and disgrace come to whoever refuses discipline, but honor comes to whoever accepts correction.
13.19: A longing fulfilled is sweet to the soul, but fools find it revolting to turn from evil.
13.20: Walk with the wise and you will become wise, but whoever befriends fools will suffer harm.
13.21: Calamity pursues sinners, but the righteous will be rewarded with good.
13.22: A good man leaves an inheritance for his grandchildren, but the wealth of a sinner is stored up for the righteous.
13.23: The land of the poor may produce abundant food, but it is swept away through injustice.
13.24: Whoever spares the rod hates his son, but whoever loves him disciplines him while there is time.
13.25: The righteous eat until they are satisfied, but the stomach of the wicked goes hungry.

14.1: A wise woman builds up her household, but a foolish woman tears hers down with her own hands.
14.2: Whoever walks in uprightness fears the LORD, but whoever is crooked in his ways despises him.
14.3: A fool's talk brings punishment upon himself, but the lips of the wise protect them.
14.4: Where there are no oxen, the stall is clean, but much increase comes through the strength of an ox.
14.5: An honest witness does not lie, but a false witness breathes out lies.
14.6: A mocker looks for wisdom but does not find it, but knowledge comes easily to a person of understanding.
14.7: Stay away from a foolish person — there is no knowledge on his lips.
14.8: The wisdom of the prudent is to understand his way, but the folly of fools is self-deception.
14.9: Fools scoff at making amends for sin, but among the upright there is goodwill.
14.10: Each heart knows its own sorrow, and no outsider can fully share its joy.
14.11: The household of the wicked will be ruined, but the tent of the upright will flourish.
14.12: There is a path that seems right to a person, but it leads to death in the end.
14.13: Even in laughter the heart may ache, and the end of joy can be sorrow.
14.14: One who turns away from faithfulness is filled with the results of his own choices, but a good man finds satisfaction within himself.
14.15: The naive person believes everything he hears, but the prudent person gives careful thought to his steps.
14.16: A wise man fears and turns from evil, but a fool is reckless and overconfident.
14.17: A quick-tempered person acts foolishly, and a person who schemes wickedly is despised.
14.18: The naive end up with folly as their inheritance, but the prudent are crowned with knowledge.
14.19: Evil people will bow before those who are good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
14.20: Even his neighbor dislikes a poor person, but a rich person has many friends.
14.21: Whoever despises his neighbor is sinning, but blessed is whoever shows kindness to the poor.
14.22: Do not those who plot evil go astray? But those who plan good find loyal love and faithfulness.
14.23: There is profit in all hard work, but mere talk leads only to poverty.
14.24: The wealth of the wise is their crown, but fools' folly is folly to the end.
14.25: A truthful witness saves lives, but a false witness deals in lies.
14.26: The fear of the LORD is a source of strong confidence, and his children will find refuge in him.
14.27: The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning one away from the traps of death.
14.28: A large population is a king's glory, but a prince is ruined by a lack of people.
14.29: Whoever is slow to anger shows great understanding, but a quick temper elevates foolishness.
14.30: A calm and contented heart brings health to the body, but envy eats away at the bones.
14.31: Whoever oppresses the poor insults their Maker, but whoever shows kindness to the needy honors him.
14.32: The wicked are brought down by their own evil, but the righteous have hope even in death.
14.33: Wisdom quietly rests in the heart of the discerning, but it makes itself known even among fools.
14.34: Righteousness lifts a nation up, but sin brings disgrace to any people.
14.35: A king's favor rests on a servant who acts wisely, but his anger falls on one who acts shamefully.

15.1: A gentle answer turns away anger, but a harsh word stirs up wrath.
15.2: The tongue of the wise presents knowledge rightly, but the mouth of fools gushes foolishness.
15.3: The LORD's eyes are everywhere, watching both the evil and the good.
15.4: A wholesome tongue is a tree of life, but a twisted tongue crushes the spirit.
15.5: A fool rejects his father's discipline, but whoever accepts correction shows good sense.
15.6: The household of the righteous holds great treasure, but the income of the wicked brings trouble.
15.7: The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but the minds of fools do not.
15.8: The LORD detests the sacrifice of the wicked, but the prayer of the upright is his delight.
15.9: The LORD detests the way of the wicked, but he loves whoever earnestly pursues righteousness.
15.10: There is harsh discipline for the one who leaves the right path, and whoever hates correction will die.
15.11: Death and Destruction lie open before the LORD — how much more the hearts of people!
15.12: A mocker does not like being corrected and will not seek the company of the wise.
15.13: A joyful heart makes a bright face, but a crushed spirit shows in the countenance.
15.14: A discerning heart is always seeking knowledge, but the mouth of a fool feeds on folly.
15.15: All the days of the afflicted are miserable, but a joyful heart provides a constant feast.
15.16: Better to have little with the fear of the LORD than great wealth with turmoil.
15.17: Better a simple meal where love is present than a lavish feast accompanied by hatred.
15.18: A hot-tempered man stirs up conflict, but whoever is slow to anger quiets it.
15.19: The path of the lazy is like a hedgerow of thorns, but the road of the upright is a level highway.
15.20: A wise son brings his father joy, but a foolish man shows contempt for his mother.
15.21: Folly is entertaining to a person who lacks judgment, but a person of understanding walks straight.
15.22: Plans fail where there is no guidance, but with many counselors they succeed.
15.23: A person finds joy in giving the right answer, and how good is a timely word!
15.24: The way of life leads upward for the wise, so that they may avoid Sheol below.
15.25: The LORD will pull down the house of the proud, but he maintains the widow's property lines.
15.26: The LORD detests the plans of the wicked, but pure words are pleasing to him.
15.27: Whoever is greedy for gain brings trouble to his own household, but whoever refuses bribes will live.
15.28: The heart of the righteous weighs its answer carefully, but the mouth of the wicked blurts out evil.
15.29: The LORD is distant from the wicked, but he hears the prayer of the righteous.
15.30: Bright eyes make the heart glad, and good news refreshes the whole body.
15.31: The ear that heeds life-giving correction will find its home among the wise.
15.32: Whoever refuses correction despises himself, but whoever accepts reproof gains insight.
15.33: The fear of the LORD is training in wisdom, and humility comes before honor.

16.1: The plans of the heart belong to man, but the right answer of the tongue comes from the LORD.
16.2: All a man's ways seem right to him, but the LORD weighs the spirits.
16.3: Commit your work to the LORD, and your plans will be established.
16.4: The LORD has made everything for its purpose, even the wicked for the day of disaster.
16.5: Everyone with a proud heart is detestable to the LORD; be assured — they will not go unpunished.
16.6: By faithful love and truth iniquity is atoned for, and by the fear of the LORD people turn from evil.
16.7: When a person's ways please the LORD, he makes even their enemies live at peace with them.
16.8: Better is a little with righteousness than great income without justice.
16.9: A man's heart plans his way, but the LORD directs his steps.
16.10: An oracle is on the lips of the king; in giving judgment his mouth does not err.
16.11: Honest scales and balances belong to the LORD; all the weights in the bag are his concern.
16.12: It is detestable for kings to commit wickedness, for a throne is made secure by righteousness.
16.13: Righteous lips are the delight of kings, and they love the one who speaks honestly.
16.14: A king's wrath is like messengers of death, but a wise person will appease it.
16.15: In the light of the king's face is life, and his favor is like a cloud of spring rain.
16.16: How much better to gain wisdom than gold, and to choose understanding over silver!
16.17: The road of the upright leads away from evil; whoever keeps to that road protects his life.
16.18: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
16.19: It is better to be humble in spirit with the poor than to share plunder with the proud.
16.20: Whoever handles a matter wisely will find good, and blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD.
16.21: The wise in heart is called discerning, and pleasant words increase the ability to learn.
16.22: Understanding is a fountain of life to those who have it, but the instruction fools give is only folly.
16.23: The heart of the wise teaches his mouth and adds persuasiveness to his words.
16.24: Pleasant words are like a honeycomb — sweet to the soul and healing to the bones.
16.25: There is a path that seems right to a person, but its end is the way of death.
16.26: A worker's appetite works for him; his hunger drives him on.
16.27: A worthless man digs up evil, and his words burn like fire.
16.28: A perverse man stirs up conflict, and a gossip drives close friends apart.
16.29: A violent man entices his neighbor and leads him down a path that is not good.
16.30: He who closes his eyes schemes perversity; he who purses his lips brings evil to pass.
16.31: Gray hair is a crown of glory; it is found on the path of righteousness.
16.32: Whoever is slow to anger is better than the mighty, and whoever rules his spirit is better than one who captures a city.
16.33: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision comes from the LORD.

17.1: Better a dry morsel in peace than a house full of feasting with strife.
17.2: A wise servant will rule over a son who causes shame and will share in the inheritance among brothers.
17.3: The crucible is for silver and the furnace for gold, and the LORD tests hearts.
17.4: An evil person listens to wicked lips, and a liar pays attention to a destructive tongue.
17.5: Whoever mocks the poor insults his Maker, and whoever gloats over disaster will not go unpunished.
17.6: Grandchildren are the crown of the elderly, and children find their glory in their fathers.
17.7: Fine speech does not suit a fool; much less do lying lips suit a ruler.
17.8: A bribe is like a charm stone in the eyes of the one who uses it; wherever he turns, he seems to succeed.
17.9: Whoever covers an offense seeks love, but whoever keeps bringing it up drives close friends apart.
17.10: A rebuke penetrates more deeply into a wise person than a hundred blows into a fool.
17.11: An evil man seeks only rebellion, and a merciless messenger will be sent against him.
17.12: Better to encounter a bear robbed of her cubs than a fool caught up in his foolishness.
17.13: Whoever returns evil for good — evil will not leave his house.
17.14: The start of conflict is like a crack letting out water; so abandon the dispute before it breaks open.
17.15: Both the one who acquits the wicked and the one who condemns the righteous are detestable to the LORD.
17.16: Why should a fool have money to buy wisdom when he has no desire for it?
17.17: A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for times of adversity.
17.18: A person who lacks good sense gives a pledge of surety in the presence of his neighbor.
17.19: Whoever loves quarreling loves transgression, and whoever raises his gate courts destruction.
17.20: Whoever has a crooked heart finds no good, and whoever has a perverse tongue falls into trouble.
17.21: Whoever fathers a fool does so to his grief, and the father of a fool has no joy.
17.22: A joyful heart is good medicine, but a broken spirit dries up the bones.
17.23: A wicked man accepts a bribe in secret to pervert the course of justice.
17.24: Wisdom is right in front of the one who has understanding, but the eyes of a fool wander to the ends of the earth.
17.25: A foolish son is grief to his father and bitterness to the mother who bore him.
17.26: It is not good to fine an innocent person, or to strike the noble simply for acting rightly.
17.27: Whoever has knowledge restrains his words, and a person of understanding has a calm spirit.
17.28: Even a fool who stays silent is considered wise, and the one who keeps his lips shut is thought to be discerning.

18.1: Whoever isolates himself pursues his own desires and disregards all sound wisdom.
18.2: A fool takes no pleasure in understanding, but only in self-expression.
18.3: When wickedness comes, contempt comes with it, and along with disgrace comes reproach.
18.4: The words of a person's mouth are deep waters, and the fountain of wisdom is a flowing stream.
18.5: It is not good to show partiality to the wicked by denying justice to the righteous.
18.6: A fool's lips walk him into arguments, and his mouth calls out for a beating.
18.7: A fool's mouth is his ruin, and his lips are a trap for his very life.
18.8: The words of a gossip are like choice morsels; they sink down into the inner depths.
18.9: Whoever is lazy in his work is kin to the one who destroys.
18.10: The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous run to it and are protected.
18.11: A rich man's wealth is his strong city, and like a high wall in his imagination.
18.12: Before destruction a person's heart is proud, but humility comes before honor.
18.13: To answer before listening is folly and disgrace.
18.14: A man's spirit can endure sickness, but a crushed spirit — who can bear it?
18.15: The discerning heart acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks more of it.
18.16: A person's gift opens doors and brings him before important people.
18.17: The first to state his case seems right, until another comes and examines him.
18.18: The lot settles disputes and parts the mighty from each other.
18.19: A brother who has been offended is harder to win back than a fortified city, and disputes between brothers are like the bars of a citadel.
18.20: A person is satisfied by the fruit of his words, and by the produce of his lips he is filled.
18.21: Death and life are in the power of the tongue, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
18.22: Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing and receives favor from the LORD.
18.23: The poor person speaks with pleas, but the rich person answers harshly.
18.24: There are companions who lead to ruin, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.

25.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied.
25.2: It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search them out.
25.3: As the heavens are high and the earth is deep, so the heart of kings is unsearchable.
25.4: Remove dross from the silver, and a vessel emerges fit for the silversmith.
25.5: Remove the wicked from before the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
25.6: Do not exalt yourself in the king's presence, and do not stand in the place of the great.
25.7: For it is better that someone say to you, 'Come up here,' than that you be put lower before a noble you have seen.
25.8: Do not rush into a dispute, lest you do not know what to do when your neighbor shames you in the end.
25.9: Argue your case directly with your neighbor, and do not disclose another's secret.
25.10: Lest one who hears it reproach you, and your ill repute never depart.
25.11: A word spoken at the right moment is like golden apples set in silver.
25.12: Like a gold earring and an ornament of fine gold is a wise reprover to an obedient ear.
25.13: Like the coolness of snow at harvest time is a faithful messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the spirit of his masters.
25.14: Like clouds and wind without rain is one who boasts of a gift he never gives.
25.15: By patience a ruler is persuaded, and a soft tongue can break a bone.
25.16: If you find honey, eat only enough for yourself, lest you have too much and vomit it.
25.17: Keep your foot rare in your neighbor's house, lest he have his fill of you and come to hate you.
25.18: A man who testifies falsely against his neighbor is a club, a sword, and a sharp arrow.
25.19: Trusting an unreliable person in a time of trouble is like a broken tooth or a lame foot.
25.20: Like one who takes away a garment in cold weather, and like vinegar poured on soda, is one who sings songs to a troubled heart.
25.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat; if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.
25.22: For you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.
25.23: The north wind brings rain, and a scowling face drives away a slanderer.
25.24: Better to live in a corner of the roof than with a quarrelsome wife in a spacious house.
25.25: Like cold water to a thirsty soul is good news from a distant land.
25.26: A righteous man who gives way before the wicked is like a muddied spring and a polluted well.
25.27: It is not good to eat too much honey, nor is it glorious to seek your own glory.
25.28: A man with no control over his spirit is like a city that is broken down and without walls.

26.1: Like snow in summer or rain at harvest, so honor is out of place for a fool.
26.2: Like a fluttering sparrow or a darting swallow, a causeless curse does not land.
26.3: A whip is for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools.
26.4: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, lest you become like him.
26.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he think himself wise.
26.6: Whoever sends a message by the hand of a fool cuts off his own feet and drinks violence.
26.7: Like the limp legs of a lame man is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
26.8: Like one who binds a stone in a sling is one who gives honor to a fool.
26.9: Like a thorn that goes into a drunkard's hand is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
26.10: Like an archer who wounds everyone is one who hires a fool or any passerby.
26.11: Like a dog that returns to its vomit is a fool who repeats his folly.
26.12: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
26.13: The slothful man says, 'There is a lion in the road; there is a lion in the streets.'
26.14: As a door turns on its hinges, so a sluggard turns on his bed.
26.15: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish; it is too much effort for him to bring it back to his mouth.
26.16: The sluggard thinks himself wiser than seven who can give a sensible answer.
26.17: Like one who grabs a dog by the ears is whoever meddles in a quarrel that is not his own.
26.18: Like a madman who throws firebrands, arrows, and death,
26.19: So is the man who deceives his neighbor and then says, 'I was only joking.'
26.20: Without wood, a fire goes out; without a gossip, a quarrel dies down.
26.21: As charcoal to embers and wood to fire, so is a quarrelsome man in kindling strife.
26.22: The words of a gossip are like delicacies; they go down into the inner chambers of the body.
26.23: Fervent lips paired with a wicked heart are like a clay pot glazed with silver dross.
26.24: Whoever hates disguises it with his lips, while storing up deceit within himself.
26.25: When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, for there are seven abominations in his heart.
26.26: Though his hatred is covered by deception, his wickedness will be exposed before the assembly.
26.27: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and whoever rolls a stone will have it roll back on them.
26.28: A lying tongue hates those it hurts, and a flattering mouth works ruin.

27.1: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
27.2: Let someone else praise you, and not your own mouth; a stranger, and not your own lips.
27.3: A stone is heavy and sand is a burden, but a fool's anger weighs more than both.
27.4: Wrath is cruel, and anger is overwhelming, but who can stand before envy?
27.5: Open rebuke is better than hidden love.
27.6: The wounds of a friend can be trusted, but an enemy's kisses are lavish and false.
27.7: One who is full despises honey, but to the hungry person every bitter thing is sweet.
27.8: Like a bird that flees its nest, so is a man who wanders from his home.
27.9: Oil and perfume bring joy to the heart; so does a friend's earnest counsel.
27.10: Do not forsake your friend or your father's friend; do not go to your brother's house in the day of your trouble. A neighbor who is near is better than a brother who is far away.
27.11: Be wise, my son, and gladden my heart, so I may have a word for whoever taunts me.
27.12: A prudent person sees danger and hides, but the simple walk on and suffer for it.
27.13: Take his garment when he has put up security for a stranger, and hold it in pledge when he does so for a wayward woman.
27.14: Whoever blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be counted a curse to him.
27.15: A constant dripping on a rainy day and a quarrelsome wife are alike.
27.16: Trying to restrain her is like trying to restrain the wind, and like trying to grasp oil in the right hand.
27.17: As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another.
27.18: Whoever tends the fig tree will eat its fruit; whoever looks after his master will be honored.
27.19: As water reflects a face, so one person's heart reflects another's.
27.20: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.
27.21: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace is for gold, and a man is tested by his praise.
27.22: Even if you grind a fool in a mortar with a pestle amid grain, his folly will not leave him.
27.23: Know well the condition of your flocks, and give careful attention to your herds.
27.24: For riches do not last forever; nor does the crown endure to all generations.
27.25: When the grass is gone and new growth appears and the mountain plants are gathered,
27.26: The lambs are for your clothing, and the goats supply the price of a field.
27.27: There will be enough goats' milk for your food, for the food of your household, and nourishment for your servant girls.

22.1: A good name is to be chosen over great riches, and favor is better than silver and gold.
22.2: The rich and the poor have this in common: the LORD made them both.
22.3: A prudent person sees danger and takes cover, but the naive keep going and suffer for it.
22.4: The reward of humility and the fear of the LORD is riches, honor, and life.
22.5: Thorns and snares are in the path of the perverse; whoever guards his life will stay far from them.
22.6: Train a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not turn from it.
22.7: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender.
22.8: Whoever sows injustice will reap trouble, and the weapon of his rage will be broken.
22.9: A generous person will be blessed, for he shares his bread with the poor.
22.10: Drive out the mocker, and conflict goes with him; strife and dishonor will stop.
22.11: Whoever loves a pure heart and whose speech is gracious will have the king as his friend.
22.12: The eyes of the LORD watch over knowledge, and he frustrates the words of the treacherous.
22.13: The sluggard says, "There is a lion outside! I will be killed in the streets!"
22.14: The mouth of an adulterous woman is a deep pit; the one under the LORD's displeasure will fall into it.
22.15: Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of discipline will drive it out.
22.16: Oppressing the poor to enrich yourself, or giving gifts to the wealthy — both lead only to poverty.
22.17: Turn your ear and listen to the words of the wise; apply your heart to my teaching.
22.18: For it is good to keep them in your heart, and to have them ready on your lips.
22.19: I am teaching you today — you specifically — so that your confidence may be in the LORD.
22.20: Have I not written down for you thirty excellent sayings of counsel and knowledge?
22.21: To teach you true and reliable words, so that you may give an accurate answer to those who send you.
22.22: Do not rob the poor because they are poor, or crush the afflicted at the city gate.
22.23: For the LORD will take up their case and will plunder those who plundered them.
22.24: Do not befriend an angry man or associate with someone hot-tempered.
22.25: Lest you learn his ways and set a trap for yourself.
22.26: Do not be one of those who shakes hands in a pledge or puts up security for loans.
22.27: If you have nothing to pay with, why let him take away your bed from under you?
22.28: Do not move the ancient boundary stone that your ancestors put in place.
22.29: Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will serve kings — not obscure people.

23.1: When you sit down to eat with a ruler, give careful thought to what is before you.
23.2: Put a knife to your throat if you have an unruly appetite.
23.3: Do not crave his delicacies, for that food is deceptive.
23.4: Do not wear yourself out trying to get rich; have the wisdom to show restraint.
23.5: Will you exhaust your eyes chasing what is not there? Riches sprout wings and fly away like an eagle into the sky.
23.6: Do not eat the bread of a stingy man, and do not covet his choice food.
23.7: For he is one who keeps a running count in his mind; he says, "Eat and drink," but his heart is not with you.
23.8: You will vomit up the little you ate, and your pleasant words will have been wasted.
23.9: Do not speak to a fool, for he will only scorn the wisdom behind your words.
23.10: Do not move an ancient boundary stone, and do not enter the fields of orphans.
23.11: For their Redeemer is strong; he will plead their case against you.
23.12: Devote your heart to instruction and your ears to words of knowledge.
23.13: Do not withhold discipline from a child; if you strike him with the rod, he will not die.
23.14: If you discipline him with the rod, you will save his life from death.
23.15: My son, if your heart is wise, then my heart will be glad as well.
23.16: Yes, my inmost being will rejoice when your lips speak what is right.
23.17: Do not let your heart envy sinners, but live in the fear of the LORD all day long.
23.18: For surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
23.19: Listen, my son, and be wise; keep your heart on the right path.
23.20: Do not be among those who drink too much wine or those who gorge themselves on meat.
23.21: For the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them in rags.
23.22: Listen to your father who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she grows old.
23.23: Buy the truth and do not sell it — wisdom, instruction, and understanding as well.
23.24: The father of a righteous child will greatly rejoice; the one who fathers a wise son will have joy from him.
23.25: Let your father and mother be glad; let the one who bore you rejoice.
23.26: My son, give me your heart, and let your eyes keep watch over my ways.
23.27: For a prostitute is a deep pit, and an immoral woman is a narrow well.
23.28: She lies in wait like a hunter and multiplies the unfaithful among men.
23.29: Who has grief? Who has anguish? Who has quarreling? Who has needless wounds? Who has bleary eyes?
23.30: Those who linger over wine and go looking for spiced wine.
23.31: Do not look at wine when it is red and sparkling in the cup, when it goes down smoothly.
23.32: In the end it bites like a snake and stings like a viper.
23.33: Your eyes will see things that are not there, and your heart will say perverse things.
23.34: You will be like one asleep in the middle of the sea, like one sprawled on top of a ship's mast.
23.35: "They beat me, but I felt no pain; they struck me, but I did not know it. When will I wake up so I can have another drink?"

24.1: Do not envy evil people or wish to be in their company.
24.2: For their heart plans violence, and their lips talk about causing trouble.
24.3: By wisdom a house is built, and by understanding it is established.
24.4: Through knowledge the rooms are filled with all kinds of precious and pleasant treasures.
24.5: A wise man is strong, and a man of knowledge grows in might.
24.6: For by wise guidance you can wage war, and victory depends on having many advisers.
24.7: Wisdom is beyond a fool's reach; he has nothing to say at the city gate.
24.8: Whoever schemes to do evil will be known as a troublemaker.
24.9: The intention behind foolish schemes is sin, and the mocker is detestable to everyone.
24.10: If you falter in times of trouble, how small is your strength!
24.11: Rescue those being taken away to death; hold back those stumbling toward slaughter.
24.12: If you say, "We did not know this," does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who guards your life know it? Will he not repay each person according to what they have done?
24.13: My son, eat honey because it is good, and the honeycomb that is sweet to your taste.
24.14: So is wisdom to your soul; when you find it, there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
24.15: Do not lie in wait against the home of the righteous, O wicked man; do not raid his dwelling.
24.16: For a righteous person falls seven times and gets up again, but the wicked are brought down by calamity.
24.17: Do not gloat when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart be glad when he stumbles.
24.18: Lest the LORD see it and be displeased, and he turns his anger away from your enemy.
24.19: Do not fret because of evildoers or be envious of the wicked.
24.20: For the evil person has no future reward; the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out.
24.21: My son, fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with those who seek to undermine either.
24.22: For their disaster will come suddenly, and who knows what destruction may come from both?
24.23: These also are sayings of the wise: showing partiality in judgment is not right.
24.24: Whoever tells the guilty, "You are innocent," will be cursed by peoples and condemned by nations.
24.25: But those who rebuke the wicked will find delight, and a generous blessing will come over them.
24.26: Whoever gives an honest answer is like a kiss on the lips.
24.27: Prepare your outdoor work and get your fields ready; after that, build your house.
24.28: Do not testify against your neighbor without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
24.29: Do not say, "I will do to him what he did to me; I will repay him for what he has done."
24.30: I passed by the field of a lazy man and the vineyard of a man who lacked sense.
24.31: It was all overgrown with thorns; the ground was covered with nettles, and the stone wall was in ruins.
24.32: I saw it and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction.
24.33: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest —
24.34: Then poverty will come on you like a wanderer, and your need like an armed man.

28.1: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a young lion.
28.2: When a land is in transgression, it has many rulers; but through a man of understanding and knowledge its stability endures.
28.3: A poor man who oppresses the poor is like a sweeping rain that leaves no food.
28.4: Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law contend with them.
28.5: Evil men do not understand justice, but those who seek the LORD understand it fully.
28.6: Better is the poor man who walks in his integrity than the one who is perverse in his ways, even if he is rich.
28.7: Whoever keeps the law is a wise son, but a companion of gluttons brings shame on his father.
28.8: Whoever increases wealth by interest and dishonest gain gathers it for one who will be generous to the poor.
28.9: Whoever turns his ear away from the law — even his prayer is detestable.
28.10: Whoever leads the righteous astray will fall into his own pit, but the blameless will receive a good inheritance.
28.11: A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man with understanding sees right through him.
28.12: When the righteous triumph, there is great rejoicing, but when the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding.
28.13: Whoever conceals his sins will not prosper, but whoever confesses and forsakes them will find mercy.
28.14: Happy is the one who fears the LORD continually, but whoever hardens his heart will fall into calamity.
28.15: Like a roaring lion or a charging bear, so is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
28.16: A ruler who lacks discernment is a great oppressor, but whoever hates unjust gain will prolong his days.
28.17: A man burdened with the guilt of another's blood will flee to the pit; let no one help him.
28.18: Whoever walks with integrity will be saved, but the one who is crooked in his ways will fall suddenly.
28.19: Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but whoever chases empty pursuits will have plenty of poverty.
28.20: A faithful man will abound with blessings, but whoever is eager to get rich quickly will not go unpunished.
28.21: To show partiality is not good, yet for a mere piece of bread a man will commit a wrong.
28.22: A man who is eager to get rich has a greedy eye and does not realize that poverty is coming for him.
28.23: Whoever rebukes a person will afterward find more favor than the one who flatters with the tongue.
28.24: Whoever robs his father or mother and says 'There is nothing wrong with it' is a partner to a man who destroys.
28.25: A greedy person stirs up strife, but whoever trusts in the LORD will prosper.
28.26: Whoever trusts his own heart is a fool, but whoever walks in wisdom will be kept safe.
28.27: Whoever gives to the poor will not lack, but whoever hides his eyes from them will get many curses.
28.28: When the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding; but when the wicked perish, the righteous multiply.

29.1: A man who is often reproved but stiffens his neck will suddenly be broken beyond remedy.
29.2: When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice; when the wicked rule, the people groan.
29.3: Whoever loves wisdom brings his father joy, but a companion of prostitutes wastes his wealth.
29.4: A king establishes the land through justice, but one who accepts bribes tears it down.
29.5: A man who flatters his neighbor spreads a net for his feet.
29.6: In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare, but the righteous sing and rejoice.
29.7: The righteous man considers the cause of the poor; the wicked man does not care to understand it.
29.8: Scornful men inflame a city, but wise men turn away wrath.
29.9: If a wise man goes to court with a fool, the fool only rages and laughs, and there is no quiet.
29.10: Bloodthirsty men hate the upright, but the righteous seek to protect his life.
29.11: A fool vents all his feelings, but a wise man quietly holds it back.
29.12: If a ruler listens to lies, all his officials become wicked.
29.13: The poor man and the oppressor have this in common: the LORD gives light to the eyes of both.
29.14: A king who judges the poor with faithfulness — his throne will be established forever.
29.15: The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother.
29.16: When the wicked increase, transgression increases, but the righteous will see their downfall.
29.17: Discipline your son, and he will give you rest; he will give delight to your soul.
29.18: Where there is no prophetic vision, the people are unrestrained; but blessed is the one who keeps the law.
29.19: A servant cannot be corrected by mere words; he understands but will not respond.
29.20: Do you see a man who is hasty in his words? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
29.21: Whoever pampers his servant from childhood will find that in the end he has become like a son.
29.22: An angry man stirs up strife, and a hot-tempered man abounds in transgression.
29.23: A man's pride will bring him low, but the one who is humble in spirit will obtain honor.
29.24: Whoever partners with a thief hates himself; he hears the curse but does not disclose it.
29.25: The fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is kept safe.
29.26: Many seek the ruler's favor, but justice for a man comes from the LORD.
29.27: An unjust man is an abomination to the righteous, and the one who walks uprightly is an abomination to the wicked.

30.1: The words of Agur son of Jakeh. The oracle. The man spoke to Ithiel, to Ithiel and Ucal:
30.2: Surely I am more brutish than any man and do not have the understanding of a human being.
30.3: I have not learned wisdom, nor have I knowledge of the Holy One.
30.4: Who has ascended to heaven and come back down? Who has gathered the wind in his fists? Who has wrapped up the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is his son's name — surely you know?
30.5: Every word of God proves true; he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.
30.6: Do not add to his words, lest he rebuke you and you be found a liar.
30.7: Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die:
30.8: Keep falsehood and lies far from me; give me neither poverty nor riches — feed me with just what I need,
30.9: lest I be full and deny you, saying 'Who is the LORD?' — or lest I be poor and steal and profane the name of my God.
30.10: Do not slander a servant to his master, lest he curse you and you be found guilty.
30.11: There is a generation that curses its father and does not bless its mother.
30.12: There is a generation that is pure in its own eyes but has not been washed from its filth.
30.13: There is a generation — how lofty are their eyes, how high their eyelids are lifted!
30.14: There is a generation whose teeth are swords and whose fangs are knives — to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among mankind.
30.15: The leech has two daughters: 'Give' and 'Give.' Three things are never satisfied; four never say 'Enough':
30.16: Sheol, the barren womb, the earth that is never satisfied with water, and the fire that never says 'Enough.'
30.17: The eye that mocks a father and scorns to obey a mother will be picked out by the ravens of the valley and eaten by the young eagles.
30.18: Three things are too wonderful for me; four I do not understand:
30.19: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent on a rock, the way of a ship in the heart of the sea, and the way of a man with a young woman.
30.20: Such is the way of an adulteress: she eats and wipes her mouth and says, 'I have done nothing wrong.'
30.21: Under three things the earth trembles; under four it cannot bear up:
30.22: a servant when he reigns, and a fool when he is full of food,
30.23: an unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maidservant who displaces her mistress.
30.24: Four things on earth are small, but they are exceedingly wise:
30.25: the ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their food in the summer;
30.26: the rock badgers are a feeble people, yet they make their homes in the cliffs;
30.27: the locusts have no king, yet all of them march out in formation;
30.28: the lizard you can take in your hands, yet it is found in kings' palaces.
30.29: Three things are majestic in their stride; four are stately in their going:
30.30: the lion, which is mightiest among beasts and does not turn back before any;
30.31: a strutting rooster, a he-goat, and a king with his army arrayed before him.
30.32: If you have been foolish, exalting yourself, or if you have devised evil, put your hand over your mouth.
30.33: For as pressing milk produces curds and pressing the nose produces blood, so pressing anger produces strife.

31.1: The words of King Lemuel, the oracle that his mother taught him.
31.2: What should I say, my son? What, son of my womb? What, son of my vows?
31.3: Do not give your strength to women, nor your vigor to those who ruin kings.
31.4: It is not for kings, O Lemuel — not for kings to drink wine, nor for rulers to desire strong drink.
31.5: Lest they drink and forget the law and pervert the judgment of all the afflicted.
31.6: Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to those who are bitter in soul.
31.7: Let him drink and forget his poverty and remember his misery no more.
31.8: Open your mouth for the mute, for the cause of all who are left desolate.
31.9: Open your mouth, judge righteously, and defend the cause of the poor and needy.
31.10: An excellent wife — who can find her? Her worth far surpasses that of rubies.
31.11: Her husband's heart trusts in her fully, and he will have no shortage of good things.
31.12: She does him good and not evil all the days of her life.
31.13: She seeks out wool and flax and works willingly with her hands.
31.14: She is like merchant ships; she brings her food from far away.
31.15: She rises while it is still dark and gives food to her household and assigns tasks to her servant girls.
31.16: She evaluates a field and buys it; from her earnings she plants a vineyard.
31.17: She girds herself with strength and makes her arms powerful.
31.18: She sees that her trade is profitable; her lamp does not go out at night.
31.19: She reaches out her hands to the distaff, and her fingers hold the spindle.
31.20: She opens her hand to the poor and extends her hands to the needy.
31.21: She has no fear of snow for her household, for all her household are clothed in warm garments.
31.22: She makes her own bed coverings; her clothing is fine linen and purple.
31.23: Her husband is known in the gates where he sits among the elders of the land.
31.24: She makes linen garments and sells them and supplies sashes to the traders.
31.25: Strength and honor are her clothing, and she faces the future with joy.
31.26: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and faithful instruction is on her tongue.
31.27: She watches over the affairs of her household and does not eat the bread of idleness.
31.28: Her children rise and call her blessed; her husband also praises her.
31.29: Many women have done excellently, but you surpass them all.
31.30: Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised.
31.31: Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the gates.
